title: Research Brief — Paramount Skydance's Acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery
subtitle: Stage-3 grounded research brief — every load-bearing fact cites a verified, source-checked claim. Analytical judgments are deferred to the IC memo.

sections.heading: Executive summary
sections.body:
On February 27, 2026, Paramount Skydance Corporation (NASDAQ: PSKY) signed a definitive merger agreement to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (NASDAQ: WBD) for **$31.00 per share in cash**, valuing WBD at roughly **$81 billion in equity value and $110 billion in enterprise value** [[deal_1]][[deal_2]]. WBD shareholders approved the merger at an April 2026 special meeting [[gov_4]]; on **June 12, 2026 the U.S. Department of Justice cleared the deal with no divestitures and no conditions** [[anti_1]][[anti_4]]. The parties target a close around Q3 2026, with a contractual outside date of **March 4, 2027** [[merg_8]].

This is the largest media combination of the decade, and it arrived only after a **contested, multi-bidder auction**. WBD had agreed in December 2025 to sell its studios-and-streaming business to **Netflix** at $27.75/share (≈$82.7B enterprise value), spinning off its linear networks [[deal_24]][[deal_25]]. Paramount went **hostile**, took an all-cash tender offer directly to shareholders [[act_1]], and escalated until the WBD board flipped, declaring Paramount's $31.00 all-cash bid a "Company Superior Proposal" on February 26, 2026 [[deal_7]]; Paramount paid WBD's **$2.8 billion termination fee to Netflix** to clear the prior deal [[deal_8]][[merg_5]].

Four threads dominate the file and frame the work for the IC memo:
- **Price.** $31.00 cash is a **147% premium** to WBD's unaffected price of $12.54 (Sept 10, 2025) [[deal_4]][[deal_5]] and sits **at the very top of the financial advisors' DCF range** ($21.16–$31.91) [[deal_15]][[deal_16]].
- **Leverage.** The all-cash deal is funded with roughly **$54–57.5 billion of new debt**, taking pro-forma leverage to ~**6–6.5x EBITDA** and total debt to ~**$79–87 billion** — financed against a target whose largest segment is in secular decline [[fin_1]][[fin_2]]. S&P will cut the combined company to junk ('BB') at close [[fin_12]].
- **Synergies.** Paramount's deal announcement sized **no** synergy figure, expressing valuation only "on a fully synergized basis" [[deal_17]][[deal_20]]; management subsequently quantified **"over $6 billion"** of cost synergies, phased ~30% in year one and 70% by year two [[fin_6]][[ops_3]].
- **Politics & regulation.** The DOJ cleared the deal unconditionally [[anti_5]], but a **threatened multi-state attorneys-general lawsuit** [[anti_12]][[anti_13]], an unresolved **FCC foreign-ownership review** (49.5% foreign ownership, 38.5% from Gulf state funds) [[anti_15]], and an **editorial-independence controversy** over common ownership of CNN and CBS News [[macro_15]] are the residual overhang.

sections.heading: The transaction
sections.body:
The headline terms, grounded in the deal press release, the Paramount 8-K, and WBD's merger proxy (DEFM14A):

| Term | Detail | |
|---|---|---|
| Consideration | $31.00 per share, all cash | [[deal_1]] |
| Equity value | ~$81 billion | [[deal_2]] |
| Enterprise value | ~$110 billion | [[deal_2]] |
| Implied multiple | 7.5x 2026 EBITDA — **on a fully synergized basis** | [[ops_2]] |
| Unaffected price | $12.54 (close, Sept 10, 2025) | [[deal_4]] |
| Premium | **147%** to unaffected ( $31.00 / $12.54 ≈ 2.47x ) | [[deal_4]][[deal_5]] |
| Last pre-announcement price | $28.80 (Feb 26, 2026) | [[deal_6]] |
| Ticking fee | $0.25/share per quarter if not closed by Sept 30, 2026 | [[deal_3]][[merg_7]] |
| Target termination fee (WBD → PSKY) | $3 billion | [[merg_3]] |
| Reverse regulatory fee (PSKY → WBD) | $7 billion | [[merg_4]] |
| Netflix fee paid by PSKY on WBD's behalf | $2.8 billion | [[merg_5]] |
| Outside date | March 4, 2027 (one automatic 3-month extension) | [[merg_8]] |
| Shareholder vote | approved, April 2026 special meeting | [[gov_4]] |
| DOJ antitrust | cleared with no conditions, June 12, 2026 | [[anti_1]][[anti_4]] |
| Financing | ~$54–57.5B new debt; no financing condition | [[fin_1]][[merg_13]] |

The deal is structured as an all-cash acquisition of 100% of WBD [[deal_1]]; because it is all-cash, former WBD holders will not participate in the combined company's future earnings or growth [[deal_11]]. A condition of Paramount's obligation to close is that WBD has **not** completed its previously planned separation into two companies [[cprof_13]].

sections.heading: The companies
sections.body:
**Warner Bros. Discovery (the target).** WBD reports three segments — **Streaming** (HBO Max, discovery+, HBO premium pay-TV), **Studios** (Warner Bros. film/TV production), and **Global Linear Networks** (U.S. and international cable networks, including CNN) [[cprof_1]]. FY2025 consolidated revenue was **$37,296 million, down 5%**, continuing a multi-year decline from $41,321M in FY2023 [[cprof_2]]; total Adjusted EBITDA was **$8,744 million, down 3%** [[cprof_7]]. The shape of the business is the central fact: the **declining linear networks are still the largest revenue and cash contributor**, while the growing streaming/studios businesses are smaller but rising.

| WBD segment (FY2025) | Revenue | YoY | Adj. EBITDA | YoY | |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Global Linear Networks | $17,656M (~47%) | −12% | $6,412M | −21% | [[cprof_5]][[cprof_8]][[cprof_6]] |
| Streaming | $12,619M (~34%) | +9% | +$693M improvement | — | [[cprof_3]][[cprof_9]][[cprof_6]] |
| Studios | $10,876M (~29%) | +5% | $2,545M | +54% | [[cprof_4]][[cprof_9]][[cprof_6]] |
| *Streaming & Studios combined* | *$20,371M* | *+3%* | *$3,496M* | *+64%* | [[cprof_10]][[cprof_11]] |

On the eve of the deal, the "melting" Global Linear Networks segment still produced roughly **1.8x the EBITDA** of the growth-engine Streaming & Studios division ($6,412M / $3,496M ≈ 1.83x) [[cprof_12]]. WBD ended 2025 with **131.6 million streaming subscribers** (+14.7M in the year) [[cprof_15]] and ~35,500 employees [[cprof_14]]. Its stated strategy is to grow streaming globally, enhance Studios, and **"manage" the linear networks** — explicitly treating linear as a business to be managed, not grown [[cprof_16]] — and it had a board-approved plan to split into two companies before Paramount intervened [[cprof_13]]. Credit-quality was already eroding: S&P, Moody's and Fitch downgraded WBD in 2025, citing linear declines and leverage [[cprof_18]]; WBD paid **$2,295M of cash interest** in 2025 [[cprof_19]].

**Paramount Skydance (the acquirer).** Formed from the 2025 Skydance/Paramount Global merger, Paramount guided to **~$30 billion of 2025 revenue** (and ~$30B in 2026, +4%) [[cprof_21]]. It exhibits the **same linear-decline / streaming-growth split** as its target: in Q3 2025 TV Media revenue fell 12% while Direct-to-Consumer grew 17%, led by a 24% rise in Paramount+ revenue [[cprof_22]]. **Paramount+ reached 79.1 million subscribers** (+13% YoY) [[cprof_23]], and management raised its run-rate cost-efficiency target from $2B to at least $3B [[cprof_24]]. Its stated strategy is explicitly to build "a global, scaled streaming platform" [[cprof_25]] — the thesis that motivates buying WBD.

sections.heading: Industry & business model
sections.body:
The combination sits at the center of the **streaming endgame**. The global video-streaming market was ~**$160 billion in 2025**, forecast to ~$195.85B in 2026 (an 18.5% CAGR to 2035) [[ind_1]], with the U.S. portion ~$47.6 billion [[ind_2]]. The business model is a subscription-scale game: content is a largely fixed cost, so profitability turns on **spreading that content spend over the largest possible subscriber base**. On that axis the market is top-heavy.

| Streamer (latest disclosed) | Subscribers | Latest-quarter streaming profit | |
|---|---|---|---|
| Netflix | 325M | +$2.42B (rev +17.6%) | [[ind_4]][[ind_10]] |
| Disney+ / Hulu (+ ESPN+) | 195.7M (+24.1M) | +$450M | [[ind_5]][[ind_11]] |
| WBD (HBO Max) | ~132M | +$393M (FY +$1.37B) | [[ind_6]][[ind_12]] |
| Paramount+ | 79M | −$158M (loss narrowing) | [[ind_8]][[ind_13]] |
| Peacock (Comcast) | 44M | unprofitable, widening losses | [[ind_9]] |

Paramount+ and HBO Max are each **sub-scale relative to Netflix and Disney**. Combined they would reach **~200M+ subscribers** (Ellison's framing) [[ind_14]] — roughly 0.65x Netflix's 325M base, so the merger **narrows but does not close** the gap to the leader [[ind_23]]. Critically, the two platforms sit at **opposite profitability points** — HBO Max profitable (~$1.37B FY), Paramount+ still loss-making — which tempers a clean "profitable density" read of the combination [[ind_24]].

**Precedent media M&A (the comparable set).** Sector mega-deals over the last decade have generally closed, with horizontal overlap remedied by divestiture rather than outright blocks:

| Deal | Year | Value | Outcome | |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AT&T / Time Warner | 2018–19 | $85.4B | DOJ sued to block, **lost on appeal**, no conditions | [[ind_21]][[anti_18]] |
| Disney / 21st Century Fox | 2019 | ~$71B | cleared with **22 regional sports network divestiture** | [[ind_17]][[anti_19]] |
| Discovery / WarnerMedia | 2022 | ~$43B | Reverse Morris Trust — **created WBD itself** | [[ind_19]][[ind_20]] |
| Comcast / Sky | 2018 | ~$39B | won a rare three-round auction | [[ind_22]] |
| Netflix / Warner Bros. | Dec 2025 | ~$82.7B | **superseded/abandoned** — the bid Paramount beat | [[ind_16]] |

This precedent set establishes a base rate in which the binding antitrust constraint has been horizontal overlap (remedied by divestiture, as with Disney's ESPN+RSNs), not vertical-integration blocks [[ind_25]][[anti_20]].

sections.heading: Deal rationale, the premium, and the Netflix flip
sections.body:
**The price is full.** $31.00 cash is a **147% premium** to the $12.54 unaffected price [[deal_4]], i.e. roughly 2.5x the pre-auction level [[deal_5]]. The fairness opinion was a **joint** opinion of Allen & Company and J.P. Morgan; notably, WBD did **not** request and Evercore did **not** render a fairness opinion [[deal_12]]. The advisors' sum-of-the-parts DCF produced an implied equity range of **$21.16–$31.91 per share** — so the $31.00 price sits **at the very top of intrinsic value**, leaving little standalone upside on the table, consistent with a contested-auction outcome rather than a discount entry [[deal_15]][[deal_16]].

**Why the board flipped from Netflix to Paramount.** The competing Netflix deal was **$27.75/share cash-and-stock** ($23.25 cash + $4.50 stock), ~$82.7B enterprise value, and acquired **only** the Streaming & Studios business after a Discovery Global spin-off [[deal_24]][[deal_25]]. Paramount's $31.00 all-cash whole-company bid delivered both **higher headline value (+11.7%)** and **cash certainty**, removing the stock-collar valuation risk the board flagged and covering 100% of the company [[deal_27]]. The WBD board's own stated reasons lead with exactly these points: an all-cash amount of at least $31.00 providing "certainty of value and liquidity" [[deal_9]], with all-cash consideration removing valuation risk [[deal_10]] — while acknowledging the trade-off that holders forgo future upside [[deal_11]].

**The synergy case — asserted, then sized.** Paramount's February 27 announcement disclosed **no** dollar synergy figure, expressing both its 7.5x EBITDA valuation and its 4.3x leverage only "on a fully synergized basis" [[deal_17]][[deal_18]], with a purely qualitative basis (technology/infrastructure streamlining) [[deal_19]] — meaning the headline multiple could not be independently checked at announcement [[deal_20]]. Management subsequently **quantified** the figure: Paramount expects **"over $6 billion" of synergies, phased ~30% in year one and 70% by year two** [[fin_6]], and CEO David Ellison reaffirmed the **$6 billion** number in March 2026, explicitly rebutting a larger figure Netflix's co-CEO had implied [[ops_4]][[ops_5]]. For contrast, the losing Netflix bid had disclosed **$2–3 billion** of annual cost savings and GAAP-EPS accretion by year two up front [[deal_26]].

sections.heading: Financing & balance sheet — the central tail risk
sections.body:
To fund an all-cash $110B deal, banks led by **Bank of America and Citigroup** committed roughly **$54–57.5 billion of new debt**, taking post-deal leverage to ~**6–6.5x EBITDA** [[fin_1]][[fin_3]]. The combined company is expected to carry **$79–87 billion of total debt** at close [[fin_2]]. The structure evolved through early 2026: a **$49.0 billion senior secured bridge** giving way to permanent financing of ~$39.5B first-lien and $12.4B second-lien secured debt [[fin_4]], with the bridge syndicated across 18 banks [[fin_9]], $10.0B of permanent secured facilities put in place, and the unsecured revolver upsized to $5.0 billion [[fin_10]][[fin_8]]. A **$43.3 billion personal guarantee from Larry Ellison** backstops the equity financing [[fin_14]].

**This will close as a junk-rated borrower.** S&P, which had already cut Paramount to junk, will lower the issuer rating to **'BB' at close** and does **not** expect leverage to fall below its 4.25x downgrade threshold until **2029** [[fin_12]]. S&P treated Ellison's verbal leverage pledge as a tacit promise to inject capital if needed, after skeptics called the post-merger leverage "frightening" [[fin_19]]. Management's deleveraging plan targets net debt/EBITDA below **3.75x by FY2028 and 3.0x by FY2029** [[fin_5]] — but that path **rests on realizing the $6B of synergies against a structurally declining base** [[fin_22]]. A near-term pressure point: WBD carries ~**$16.5 billion of debt maturing in 2027** (within ~$32.7B of scheduled repayments), which the combined company must term out at junk spreads — partly pre-empted by refinancing WBD's $15B bridge [[fin_17]][[fin_23]][[fin_18]]. Liquidity is supported by the $5.0B revolver plus WBD's ~$4.6B cash [[fin_16]][[fin_23]].

sections.heading: Merger agreement & deal protections
sections.body:
The risk-allocation terms are markedly **seller-favorable**, signalling high buyer determination to close (from the DEFM14A "The Merger Agreement" summary and the Paramount 8-K):

| Provision | Term | |
|---|---|---|
| Company termination fee (WBD → PSKY) | $3 billion | [[merg_3]] |
| Reverse regulatory fee (PSKY → WBD) | **$7 billion** | [[merg_4]] |
| Netflix fee paid up front by PSKY | $2.8 billion (recoverable only on a WBD-side termination) | [[merg_5]][[merg_6]] |
| Ticking consideration | $0.25/share per quarter after Sept 30, 2026 | [[merg_7]] |
| Outside date | March 4, 2027 + one automatic 3-month extension | [[merg_8]][[merg_9]] |
| Closing conditions | WBD vote, HSR/regulatory clearances, no restraining order | [[merg_1]] |
| Regulatory-efforts standard | **reasonable best efforts, capped — NOT hell-or-high-water** | [[merg_10]][[merg_11]] |
| Financing condition | none — PSKY reps it will have the funds | [[merg_13]] |
| Specific performance | available to WBD, no bond required | [[merg_14]] |
| Deal protection | no-shop with a fiduciary out for a Superior Proposal | [[merg_15]][[merg_16]] |
| MAC/MAE | no Company MAE since Sept 30, 2025 represented | [[merg_2]] |
| Sponsor backstop | Ellison Parties' guarantee delivered at signing | [[merg_17]] |

The package shifts regulatory and timing risk decisively onto the buyer: the $7B reverse fee is ~**2.3x** the $3B WBD would owe, Paramount fronted the $2.8B Netflix fee, waived any financing condition, and granted specific performance [[merg_18]]. **But there is an important limit:** the regulatory-efforts covenant is a "reasonable best efforts" standard **expressly capped** so Paramount need not accept divestitures or remedies amounting to a material adverse effect — so its obligation to litigate or divest to clear antitrust review is bounded, a meaningful qualifier on close-certainty even with $7B at stake [[merg_19]].

sections.heading: Antitrust & regulatory
sections.body:
**Federal antitrust risk is resolved.** On **June 12, 2026 the DOJ Antitrust Division cleared the deal**, stating it "is not likely to result in harm to competition or American consumers" [[anti_1]]; its eight-month review of 2M+ documents concluded the deal could even **strengthen** competition across streaming, linear TV and theatrical, with the combined company still facing larger rivals (Netflix, Amazon, Disney) [[anti_2]][[anti_3]]. Crucially the clearance was **unconditional — no divestitures, no behavioral remedies** [[anti_4]][[anti_5]]. The HSR path ran December 8, 2025 (filing) → Second Request → substantial-compliance certification Feb 9 → waiting period expired **February 19, 2026** [[anti_6]][[anti_7]]. Internationally, **Australia's ACCC cleared** the deal [[anti_8]][[anti_9]] and approvals came from a dozen-plus jurisdictions, though the **U.K. CMA opened a probe** that remained pending [[anti_10]][[anti_11]].

**Two regulatory risks remain live.** First, a **threatened multi-state attorneys-general lawsuit**: Bloomberg reported lawyers in ~10 states drafting a complaint, with California's AG confirming the deal "remains under investigation," on a theory centered on the merged entity's leverage over creators and workers; state AGs retain **independent authority** to challenge even after DOJ clearance, and Senator Warren urged them to do so [[anti_12]][[anti_13]][[anti_14]]. Second, an **FCC foreign-ownership review**: Paramount disclosed total foreign ownership of **49.5%, including 38.5% from Saudi, Qatari and Abu Dhabi funds**, requiring an FCC waiver above the 25% threshold [[anti_15]]; Senate Democrats warned of an "unprecedented degree of foreign control of U.S. broadcasting" [[anti_16]] and the FCC's lone Democrat called for "rigorous review" [[anti_17]].

**Base rate.** The precedent set supports closing: AT&T–Time Warner cleared over a DOJ court loss with no conditions, and Disney–Fox cleared via a 22-RSN divestiture — i.e. media mega-deals close, with state-AG litigation and the pending FCC/CMA reviews, not federal antitrust, now the residual gating risks [[anti_20]].

sections.heading: Management, governance & control
sections.body:
**Control concentrates in one person.** David Ellison holds **100% of the Ellison family's voting interests**, is Chairman and CEO, and is sole manager of the family entities that control National Amusements and the combined Paramount [[gov_9]]; the family's vehicle owns 77.5% of NAI (RedBird 22.5%) and appoints up to five of seven NAI directors [[gov_10]][[gov_11]]. The leadership bench is set: **Jeff Shell** (ex-RedBird, ex-NBCUniversal) as president [[gov_12]], **Cindy Holland** (ex-Netflix) over streaming [[gov_13]], **George Cheeks** over TV Media [[gov_14]] — and, notably, WBD's **David Zaslav is slated to stay** as President & CEO of the combined Streaming & Studios business [[gov_8]]. The combination therefore carries concentrated **key-person/control risk** around Ellison, who personally fronts the editorial-independence pledges [[gov_19]].

**The Zaslav payout is a governance flashpoint.** WBD's proxy quantifies Zaslav's golden-parachute compensation at **$886,836,175** — including $517M of equity and a **$335M tax gross-up** [[gov_1]]. The bulk vests on a **single trigger** (closing alone, regardless of employment) [[gov_2]]; the gross-up was added days before the proxy and drew Glass Lewis criticism [[gov_5]]. Shareholders **voted down** the (advisory, non-binding) pay proposal even as they approved the merger — so the package is payable anyway [[gov_3]][[gov_4]][[gov_20]]. Zaslav also sold $114M of WBD stock shortly after Paramount won [[gov_6]], and the proxy discloses the Ellisons had signaled a "several hundred million dollar" package to him during the process [[gov_7]].

**Foreign ownership and editorial independence** are the distinct governance pressure point: the 49.5% foreign / 38.5% Gulf-fund profile triggers the FCC waiver and Senate objection, against which Paramount's only mitigant is the Ellison family's retained voting control and the investors' asserted passivity [[gov_15]][[gov_16]][[gov_21]]. Amid concern over common ownership of CNN and CBS News, Ellison **pledged** CNN's editorial independence "needs to be maintained" [[gov_17]] — an assurance critics question, citing his $150M purchase of The Free Press and installation of Bari Weiss atop CBS News [[gov_18]].

sections.heading: Operational profile & integration
sections.body:
The synergy lever runs through **streaming-platform and back-office consolidation**, not content or headcount cuts. Paramount plans to **combine Paramount+ and HBO Max into one service** (preserving the HBO brand) if the deal closes [[ops_8]][[ops_9]], targeting **up to $6 billion** of cost synergies over three years that chief strategy officer Andy Gordon says will **not** include layoffs or content reductions [[ops_3]]. The asserted playbook is the tech-stack convergence Paramount is already running internally (merging Paramount+, BET+ and Pluto onto one stack) as a "rehearsal" for WBD [[ops_6]][[ops_7]] — so the synergy thesis is **execution-dependent on an integration the company has only begun** [[ops_13]].

Three facts temper the upside. First, the ~200M combined-subscriber figure **overstates net reach**: Antenna estimates **7.6M U.S. subscribers already hold both** services, and Parrot Analytics finds an HBO Max viewer is 86.7% as likely to watch a Paramount+ title as a core P+ viewer — so deduplication onto one app carries **subscriber-attrition risk** rather than pure additive growth [[ops_10]][[ops_12]][[ops_14]]. Second, the "no layoffs" framing sits in tension with management's own acknowledgment that corporate overhead "will absolutely have to" be rationalized — both companies having already absorbed prior layoff waves, raising the question of how much non-content overhead remains to cut [[ops_15]]. Third, as of the March 2026 announcement Paramount had **not disclosed pricing or naming** for the combined app [[ops_9]].

sections.heading: Macro & political overhang
sections.body:
**The secular decline of linear is the macro fact that matters.** WBD's FY2025 distribution revenue fell 8% on a 9% drop in domestic linear subscribers, and advertising revenue fell 11% on **25% linear audience declines** — so the demand base for the Global Linear Networks segment Paramount is levering up to buy is eroding at a **double-digit annual pace** [[macro_1]][[macro_2]][[macro_16]]. Independent data corroborates: U.S. pay-TV penetration has fallen to ~50% (from ~80% in 2018), with households down 6% YoY [[macro_7]][[macro_8]]. WBD itself warns there is **no assurance** streaming growth will replace structural linear decline [[macro_3]]. The combined entity is also exposed to **advertising cyclicality** [[macro_4]][[macro_5]] and, given ~$50B+ of new debt, to **rising interest rates** via its variable-rate bridge [[macro_6]].

**Politics is a genuine, two-sided transmission channel.** The deal is bankrolled by Trump ally Larry Ellison, whose son runs Paramount, and CNN reports the close ties to the administration as a controversy [[macro_11]]. That alignment **plausibly eased** the DOJ's no-conditions clearance, but it simultaneously creates residual risk — the threatened state-AG challenge [[macro_12]], the unresolved FCC foreign-ownership review [[macro_9]][[macro_10]], and a **post-close editorial-independence controversy** over commonly owned CNN and CBS News, sharpened by the FCC "capitulation over courage" dissent and Paramount's prior overhaul of CBS News / "60 Minutes" [[macro_13]][[macro_14]][[macro_15]][[macro_17]].

sections.heading: The contest & the bear case
sections.body:
There is **no classic short-seller report** on this situation; the analogue shareholder-pressure dynamic was a **two-sided contested takeover** [[act_1]][[act_13]]. After WBD agreed to Netflix, Paramount went hostile, urged shareholders to vote against the Netflix deal and to tender [[act_2]][[act_3]]; the WBD board pushed back, **reaffirming Netflix and recommending shareholders reject Paramount's "inferior" bid** [[act_4]][[act_5]], and claimed 93%+ of shareholders had rejected it [[act_6]] — until the board reversed when Paramount escalated to $31.00 [[act_7]].

**The most developed bear thesis is a leverage/financeability case** — and it came from the WBD board itself while opposing the bid. WBD argued Paramount (~$14B market cap) was funding **~$94.65 billion** of debt and equity — nearly 7x its own market cap [[act_8]] — in what it called "the largest LBO in history," with ~$87B of pro-forma gross debt and ~**7x 2026E EBITDA before synergies** [[act_9]][[act_10]]. Because the proponent was an interested party, the file corroborates the thesis directionally from two independent directions: **Netflix walked away** at $31, stating the deal was "no longer financially attractive" [[act_11]], and **Paramount itself cast doubt on the value of WBD's cable/linear assets** even as it bid for the whole company [[act_12]][[act_14]].

sections.heading: Litigation & legal
sections.body:
The litigation profile is **process- and deal-driven, not operational**. The contested process generated several suits: **Paramount itself sued WBD's board** in the Delaware Court of Chancery during the auction (expedition denied) [[lit_1]][[lit_2]]; a stockholder suit alleged 14D-9 disclosure failures [[lit_3]]; and a merger-objection suit (*Nicosia*) plus **15 stockholder demand letters** target the proxy [[lit_4]][[lit_5]]. WBD also carries standing pre-merger litigation — a securities class action (*Collura*, SDNY, Nov 2024) and a stayed derivative action [[lit_6]][[lit_7]] — and flags that further deal-related suits may be filed [[lit_8]]. A key contract underpinning certainty is the **Ellison/trust guarantee** of $45.72B of merger consideration [[lit_9]].

These disclosure/process claims are typically settled via supplemental disclosure rather than capable of blocking a deal; the genuine close risk is the **threatened multi-state attorneys-general antitrust action**, which — coming after a no-conditions DOJ clearance — is the one piece of litigation that could seek to **enjoin closing** [[lit_10]][[lit_11]][[lit_12]].

sections.heading: Preliminary read & coverage
sections.body:
**What the facts establish (for the IC memo to weigh).** This is a strategically coherent — but **expensively financed** — bid to build a No. 2 streaming platform by consolidating two sub-scale streamers and two major studios. The price is full: $31.00 is a 147% premium and sits at the top of the advisors' DCF range [[deal_5]][[deal_16]], so the economics lean on realizing **"over $6B"** of cost synergies [[fin_6]] while carrying ~$79–87B of debt at junk ratings against a **declining linear core** [[fin_2]][[fin_12]][[macro_16]]. **Deal certainty splits cleanly:** the vote is done [[gov_4]] and federal antitrust is cleared unconditionally [[anti_5]], but residual close risk concentrates in the **state-AG challenge** and the **FCC foreign-ownership review** [[anti_12]][[anti_15]] — against which Paramount's $7B reverse fee and high determination [[merg_18]] are offset by a **bounded, non-hell-or-high-water** efforts covenant [[merg_19]]. The "will it close" and "is the price/leverage sound" questions should be argued separately in the memo.

**Coverage.** 11 of 12 checklist areas are covered with verified claims; the one not searched — ESG/environmental — was deliberately scoped out (immaterial environmental liability for a media company; the live social/governance risks are captured under governance and macro). The coverage map reports **0 required/recommended gaps**, and no covered area was flagged thin.

**Honest limitations from the research pass:**
- **Synergy basis:** the $6B figure is grounded as management's assertion (with a 30%/70% phasing), but the filings give **no cost-vs-revenue split and no dollar breakdown** by tech vs. overhead vs. content — so the precise mechanism remains a reasoned judgment for the memo, not a cited number [[ops_13]].
- **Pro-forma leverage** is reported at ~6–6.5x by independent sources [[fin_1]] vs. ~4.3x management framing — the latter expressed "on a fully synergized basis"; the management 4.3x claim and its reconciliation **quarantined** on sourcing (the figure survives via S&P's verified "not below 4.25x until 2029" [[fin_12]]).
- **FCC status:** an earlier 2025 FCC broadcast-license approval (2-1, with a dissent) related to the **Skydance/CBS** transaction, **not** this WBD deal; for the WBD deal the FCC foreign-ownership review was **still pending** as of June 2026 — the brief treats it as unresolved [[anti_15]][[anti_17]].
- **Quarantines (5 of 220 proposals):** Paramount's own segment breakdown and one management-leverage datapoint quarantined on sourcing; both are backstopped by verified claims elsewhere (acquirer revenue [[cprof_21]]; leverage [[fin_1]][[fin_12]]), so no load-bearing fact was lost.
